title: JVM Internals and GC
learningObjectives: Explain JVM Internals and GC with advanced interview clarity
Implement JVM Internals and GC in Java with clean design
Avoid high-frequency failure patterns in JVM Internals and GC
Apply this concept to production-oriented scenarios
Communicate decisions with concise technical reasoning

sections.title: Why JVM Internals and GC matters
sections.content:
JVM/GC understanding helps diagnose latency, memory pressure, and production stability issues.

Day 37 is an inflection point between writing code that "works" and writing code that scales in maintainability and production reliability.

Interviewers at this stage evaluate:
1. Internal understanding (how it works, not only syntax)
2. Trade-off reasoning (why this approach over alternatives)
3. Runtime impact (performance, memory, contention, failure behavior)

This topic should be explained with one minimal example, one realistic failure mode, and one mitigation strategy.

If you can defend your design choices clearly, you will stand out from syntax-only answers.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - JVM Internals and GC
sections.content:
### Day 37: JVM Internals and GC
JVM/GC understanding helps diagnose latency, memory pressure, and production stability issues.

### Deep understanding goal
You should understand the mechanism, its boundaries, and when to avoid it.

### Practical workflow
1. Implement baseline.
2. Add correctness checks and edge handling.
3. Evaluate runtime cost.
4. Refactor for readability and team adoption.

### Interview quality bar
- Explain what problem it solves.
- Show concise Java example.
- Name one pitfall and one mitigation.
- State complexity/runtime implication.
- Compare with one alternative.

### Production angle
In backend systems, this concept influences throughput, reliability, and debugging speed. A good implementation must support operations, not just local correctness.

### Quick self-check
- Can I explain this in <60 seconds?
- Can I code it from memory?
- Can I describe a failure scenario and fix?

### Satyverse — deeper path (theory → practice)
**Security manager** deprecated/removal trajectory — understand policy files historically; modern apps use OS/container boundaries + library hardening.

**TLS:** prefer TLS 1.2+; certificate pinning for mobile; never disable verification in prod.

**Secrets:** not in source — env + vaults; rotate keys; least privilege IAM.

**Dependency scanning:** OWASP dependency-check / Snyk in CI — supply chain attacks are real interview topics.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Memorizing JVM Internals and GC instead of implementing examples.
Skipping edge cases and failure path tests.
Not explaining runtime behavior in interviews.

sections.title: Exercise - JVM Internals and GC Applied Drill
sections.problem:
Implement a Java solution demonstrating **JVM Internals and GC** in a realistic scenario.

Requirements:
1. Include one baseline approach and one improvement.
2. Handle one edge/failure case.
3. Add short explanation of trade-off.
4. Keep code readable and testable.
5. Show sample output.
hints: Use explicit naming for intent clarity.
Write one failure-case test and expected result.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is JVM heap?
conceptual.answer: Runtime data area for object allocation—Garbage Collector manages reclamation.

conceptual.answer: Stack per-thread frames with local vars; heap shared objects—escape analysis may stack-allocate.

wrongAnswers: Garbage collection runs only when System.gc() is called.
Stack memory holds all Java objects.
Metaspace is fixed size and never grows.
More heap always reduces GC time.
The JIT compiles all bytecode to native code before any execution.
WeakReference prevents an object from ever being garbage collected.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Area | Phase 4 Focus | Quick Rule | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concept | JVM Internals and GC | Explain mechanism + boundary | Syntax-only answers |
| Runtime | Performance/memory/concurrency | Mention impact explicitly | Hidden runtime costs |
| Code | Clean composable units | Prefer readability first | Over-chained complexity |
| Failure | Known pitfall + mitigation | Name one concrete case | Hand-wavy debugging |
| Interview | Decision reasoning | Compare alternatives | No trade-off discussion |
